channel.title: Comments for page "Your Future is Bright"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Your Future is Bright"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Underrated.
+1

item.title: Revisions
item.content:encoded: Hopefully my last major revision of this article. I'll be releasing more content soon enough folks, schedule is a bit nuts but things are progressing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it.
Nobody ever talks about HOW cognitohazard checks work.
And this actually makes sense. It's not a failed attempt.
The dialogue to me seemed like it was professional and rapid-succession like it should be, not a bare skeleton.
But I guess I'm not experienced here like the other people. I only got the GOI references, so I'm a little shallow into it still.
I liked it though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Android replicants.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks a bunch! I think sometimes people forget that if lots of these do exist in the same universe, the Foundation would have to rigorously test their agents for exposure. Nothing like an internal containment breach like 3333.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ooh, that was a fun watch, and quite similar. If I remember correctly, those tests were for checking for emotional deviations, right? Cause they're clone servants or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels a lot like like the baseline test from Blade Runner 2049. Just with tons of SCP references. And also less tense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It has been improved. Updoot should arrive within three to five business days.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this! The calm cockiness of Markus that you know is going to be upended in *some* way is written well, and the almost anti-climactic smack at the end was a enjoyable contrast! I almost wish that the line about the interrogator pausing wasn't there, because I pretty much gathered at that point that things had just changed for our protagonist in the worst way, so while he didn't know, we as the reader did, and I liked only knowing what he did. (edit: oh, that section has been changed since I read it earlier…this is better, but it's still pretty clear something is up)

STILL! A good read, and man, the correct answers to some of those questions make the Foundation seem like a crazy cult! Which I kind of dig…?

+1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wanted it to be a little facetious.

Then again, you novoted :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If this was downvoted, I would have called this "your future is dark"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: how bout me now, senpai?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
People saying the ending was a gut punch…? Mmmmmm, ehhhhh, it didn't feel that way to me. The ending felt pretty weak, actually. That can describe the whole piece, really. It's a little hollow. Not much description, not much character development, not much… well just not much. Some things I think that might amp up the "characterisation" and "description" here that others have complained about:

"Ever dream of a carousel?"

Plenty of people have dreams of carousels. I might have a tangent here about how he did have a dream but through interview it turns out it's completely mundane. Maybe let him get a little pissed about the tangent. Characterisation.

The tensions rose rapidly, then quickly faded away.

Let the interviewer (who the agent called a "friend") have a… lilt in his voice after this. Or something. Something that the main character can notice but not comment on, and that he continues to worry about. Description.

That's just two suggestions. Not sure how to make the ending stronger — though just having a better main article might make the end stronger through making us care more about the character.

A downvote for now, may change with edits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks Mag, I'll have to think about it more.

Maybe the first chapter will be them on a mission of some sort, and it all goes off without a hitch until the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That may be the thematic point, but from a writing perspective, in my eyes, this is a huge weakness.

This becomes forgettable quickly, because there's nothing making me care about faceless dude getting popped for an infection.

This doesn't do a lot, or much of anything, for me as a reader. It's just more MTF guys or Agent guys being chewed up by a skip.

The conversation itself isn't bad, but it can't carry an entire article to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Isn't that the point, though?

It implies that all agents go through decontamination procedures. This random fella was the unlucky one.

I'm thinking about what I can do to expand on it without it being superfluous.

The whole point is to not really see the whole picture

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I said, you need description. Why even name a character, if they're not going to be real beyond a name?

He might as well be Agent FacelessMcDoesn'tHaveKids. A name, a face, a description. Where are they? What are the interviewers like? How are they holding themselves?

Basically, you have a lot of talking, without much action. This entire tale could have taken place over walkie talkies. We have no insight in to what's actually happening beyond this list of questions and answers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I took out the links for now, because maybe that's for the best. I'm glad you liked it, I'm wondering if it really needs any additions

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this, and the links helped. The ending was a real gutpunch. We don't know just what happened, except that it was not good. Life in the Foundationverse - I'm glad I'm not there.

I'm not, am I?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understand your point. Do you have any suggestions on how to expand on this further?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd like to make a few tales for crosslinking, I don't necessarily believe that a tale needs to introduce new anomalies, and the aggression and thoroughness of a decontamination interrogation thing has never been touched upon.

I really value your opinion, so anything you think could be used to make it worthwhile would be greatly appreciated

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Short isn't the problem.

The complete lack of description is. I have no context. These might as well be literally faceless people, sitting in a void, talking, and then one is escorted away.

The actual length of this is very short, but there's no actual actions. It's a bunch of back and forth questions.

It doesn't lend itself to interesting reading, to me, at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Removing the links would reduce what I consider to be visual clutter, but you're still left with the fact that this narrative is exceedingly reliant on references to other stuff. So really, if you're looking to keep the soul of this thing intact, all that would accomplish is reducing informational content.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How would you propose I expand on it? I was under the impression that short tales can do just fine. Even a small glimpse into a large world can leave a lasting impact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was told the links would be good for newbies, but I can remove them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You need to figure out if you want this to be in the past tense or present tense, and then make the entire article consistent, because there's no reason for the shift after the first section.

The crosslinks feel shoehorned, to the point of distraction. I don't really see any narrative reason for the links mentioned, aside from winking at the userbase and going "hey guys, remember that one popular article? Good times." And given how thin the base narrative is, this is coming across as little more than liner notes for a greatest hits album.

And also:

The world turns and the heavens gaze upon the Earth with a tear down its face.

This line is awful. It's extremely out of place with the rest of the article's prose, and cringe-inducingly overwrought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The lack of context really hurts here.

Talking heads can work, but just plan, double spaced dialogue back and forth with the barest of dialogue tags doesn't work for me, unfortunately.

This is a great skeleton for a good tale, but right now, it's just an outline or concept in my eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am honored. Thanks everyone

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing this. There's everything here necessary to upvote, and very little holding it back. It's short, but it uses it to its advantage. Well done Billith.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short, sweet with a gutpunch at the end. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Short tale, maybe people will like it, maybe not

Expanded for contextual purposes. A spiritual tangent from BWBWBWBWBWG and RE: You Want Happy Endings? A bittersweet sentiment from the folks with their thankless jobs.

In the twilight, the black and white goes gray

+spoilers
-

1173
2316
2571
2719
2998
370
033/SCP-033-033/3125
093
3335

GoIs mentioned: Church of the Broken God, Fithism, and Sarkism

Headcanon: Foundation operatives that deal with coghaz situations are created tests unique for them in order to determine if they have been infected. The "Does the Black Moon Howl?" trigger phrase is unique to every agent (because there are so many answers out there.)
